babySize: the size of a poppy seed
babyDevelopment: Week 1 is calculated from the first day of your last menstrual period. While conception hasn't occurred yet, your body is preparing for ovulation.

babySize: the size of a poppy seed
babyDevelopment: Ovulation takes place this week — an egg is released from the ovary and is ready for fertilization. This is your most fertile window.

babySize: the size of a poppy seed
babyDevelopment: The fertilized egg develops into a blastocyst and implants into the uterine wall. Your body begins producing hCG, the pregnancy hormone.

babySize: the size of a sesame seed
babyDevelopment: The embryo now consists of three cell layers that will form all organs and tissues. A home pregnancy test may show a positive result.

babySize: the size of a sesame seed
babyDevelopment: The neural tube — the foundation of the brain and spinal cord — begins to form. The tiny heart of the embryo starts beating this week.
Arm and leg buds emerge, and the eyes and ears begin to take shape. The heart now beats at a steady rhythm of 100 to 160 beats per minute.
The brain develops rapidly, and tiny fingers and toes begin to form. The umbilical cord is now connecting the embryo to the placenta.
All major organs — the liver, kidneys, and lungs — begin to develop. Facial features become more distinct, and eyelids start to form.
The embryo makes its first tiny movements, though you won't feel them yet. The elbows and fingers are now forming.
The embryonic period ends — your baby is now officially a fetus. All major organs and systems are in place and will continue to mature.
Your baby moves actively and develops reflexes — they can suck their thumb and swallow. The vocal cords begin to form.
Fingers and toes are now fully separated, and tiny fingernails begin to grow. The external genitals are starting to develop, though it's still early to determine the sex.
The vocal cords continue developing, and your baby can now taste the amniotic fluid. Bones and joints are actively strengthening.
Your baby starts producing urine, which is released into the amniotic fluid. Fine hair called lanugo begins to appear on the head.
The skin is still very thin, with blood vessels visible through it. Your baby moves energetically and can turn their head.
At this stage, an ultrasound can often reveal the baby's sex. Facial muscles develop, allowing your baby to practice frowning and smiling.
Fat begins to form beneath the skin, helping with temperature regulation after birth. Your baby swallows amniotic fluid, exercising their digestive system.

babySize: the size of a bell pepper
babyDevelopment: Your baby can now hear sounds from the outside world. Bones continue to harden, and unique fingerprints are forming on their tiny fingers.
A waxy coating called vernix caseosa protects your baby's delicate skin from the amniotic fluid. The grasp reflex is developing.
This is a major milestone — the anatomy scan around week 20 allows a detailed assessment of all organs. You'll likely start feeling your baby's movements soon.
Your baby's digestive system gets a workout as they swallow amniotic fluid, which passes through the intestines. A sleep-wake cycle begins to emerge.
Your baby actively responds to your voice and loud sounds. The eyelids begin to open, and eyebrows and lashes become more visible.
The lungs start producing surfactant, a substance essential for breathing after birth. Your baby's skin becomes less transparent as fat accumulates.
Weighing about 650 grams, your baby's chances of survival outside the womb begin to increase. The brain is actively forming new neural connections.
Your baby can now distinguish between light and dark — the eyelids may flutter open. Lung tissue continues to develop actively.

babySize: the size of a head of cauliflower
babyDevelopment: The lungs continue to mature, though your baby couldn't breathe independently yet. Responses to sounds become more purposeful.

babySize: the size of a large eggplant
babyDevelopment: Your baby regularly opens and closes their eyes and can perceive bright light shining on your belly. Taste preferences begin to form.

babySize: the size of a small pumpkin
babyDevelopment: From this point onward, your baby is considered viable — if born prematurely, they have a high chance of survival. The brain continues to develop rapidly.

babySize: the size of a large pumpkin
babyDevelopment: Your baby accumulates subcutaneous fat needed for temperature regulation after birth. Movements become more coordinated and purposeful.

babySize: the size of a large head of cabbage
babyDevelopment: Your baby practices breathing by making rhythmic chest movements. The hair on their head becomes thicker, and fingernails reach the fingertips.
Your baby can turn their head from side to side. All five senses are actively functioning, and they respond to touch on your belly.
Subcutaneous fat continues to build rapidly. The skin becomes pink and smooth as lanugo — the fine body hair — begins to disappear.

babySize: the size of a large pineapple
babyDevelopment: Your baby typically moves into a head-down position in preparation for birth. The skull bones remain flexible to ease passage through the birth canal.
The nervous system continues to mature, and your baby sucks their thumb frequently to strengthen the sucking reflex. Most babies are now in a head-down position.

babySize: the size of a small melon
babyDevelopment: The kidneys are fully developed and functioning, and the lungs are nearly ready for independent breathing. Your baby continues to gain weight steadily.

babySize: the size of a small watermelon
babyDevelopment: Your baby is considered full-term: the lungs, liver, and immune system are mature enough. They could breathe independently if born now.

babySize: the size of a medium watermelon
babyDevelopment: Pregnancy is now considered full-term — labor at this stage is not premature. Your baby continues to build fat stores for insulation after birth.

babySize: the size of a large watermelon
babyDevelopment: Your body is preparing for labor, and your baby settles into their final position in the pelvis. All organs and systems are fully ready for independent life.

babySize: the size of a very large watermelon
babyDevelopment: Your baby gains about 200 grams per week at this stage. Movements may feel less vigorous as space in the uterus becomes limited.

babySize: the size of a large pumpkin
babyDevelopment: You've reached week 40 — the full term of pregnancy. Your baby is completely ready for birth, with fully mature lungs, nervous system, and immune system.
